bucket3.reason: rests on unstated input(s): midpoint_divisor — constant to compute the midpoint of the stated 4-6 month range as a single representative lead time

recs.armA: HOLD Option (c) — acuity-targeted fast lane (dedicated credentialed subspecialty capacity via vendor carve-out) plus renegotiated routine renewal — with two operational fixes that make the plan shippable against the 6-month clock. Both challenger objections are material to EXECUTION, not to the core choice, so I correct them without switching. FIX 1 (parallel timeline, replaces the 30-day gating sequence): Diagnosis is NO LONGER a gate that blocks other tracks. On Day 0, launch three parallel tracks: (A) root-cause decomposition (queue-wait vs. read-time vs. reader-availability/credentialing) targeted to complete in ~30 days; (B) RFP for a dedicated-capacity carve-out with the hardened spec — issued immediately, since vendor onboarding/stroke-center credentialing consumes weeks; (C) contingent recruitment + credentialing pipeline started NOW, given the GIVEN 4-6 month recruit lead time in a tight market, structured so it can be paused with limited sunk cost (early-stage sourcing, not signed offers) if diagnosis and RFP land on a clean routing fix. The diagnosis then SELECTS among already-warmed options rather than serially triggering them. This preserves renegotiation leverage against the 6-month renewal and the proposed 22% increase: we enter vendor talks with a credible, in-flight BATNA. FIX 2 (correct the in-house FTE math): the problem itself states 3 FTEs is 'hard to staff 365 nights' — i.e., the given 3-FTE plan is by the problem's own admission not sustainable for 365-night coverage once vacation/CME/sick/recruiting-gap and cross-site subspecialty coverage are considered. I am NOT asserting a specific higher FTE count; the true sustainable number is UNDETERMINED from the givens and MUST be derived by the client via a formal coverage/relief model (nights to cover, per-radiologist annual night capacity net of leave, subspecialty credentialing constraints) before any in-house cost comparison. Treat any in-house headcount above 3 as an ASSUMPTION the reader must verify, not a fact. The reliable, given-grounded conclusion is directional only: because 3 FTEs is stated as insufficient, real in-house cost exceeds 3 x $520k = $1.56M/yr, raising the feasibility bar and, as the challenger notes, more often routing the fallback toward a DEDICATED-CAPACITY VENDOR CONTRACT rather than DIY in-house — which reinforces (c)'s vendor-carve-out core. DECISION RULE (final): IF root-cause is primarily routing OR a vendor commits dedicated credentialed subspecialty capacity meeting the premium 20-min tier on the critical modalities (CTA head/neck, CT perfusion, emergent MRI, complex trauma) AND (carrier rewards the acuity fix OR carve-out cost < premium-on-all-9,000-studies) -> execute (c). IF binding constraint is capacity AND no vendor commits credentialed dedicated capacity at acceptable cost -> fall back: (b) only if its +31% premium tier itself commits dedicated readers (not mere priority); else a dedicated-capacity contract; else 24/7/365 in-house costed at a client-verified sustainable FTE count (which the givens confirm is greater than 3). Reject the naive 3-FTE in-house and the flat 22% increase. Net: (c) holds; the fixes make it executable within the renewal window, and the honest treatment of in-house headcount only strengthens the vendor-capacity path.
recs.armB: Adopt Option (c): bring the stroke/trauma subset (~15% of volume, ~1,350 studies/year) in-house on a fast-track basis while keeping routine overnight reads outsourced with the incumbent. Rationale: The core problem is not average cost but SLA failure on the high-acuity, high-liability minority where minutes change outcomes; concentrating scarce, expensive radiologist capacity there directly addresses the clinical and malpractice exposure the ED chief and carrier are flagging. Option (a) full in-house is not defensible: staffing 365 nights to SLA cannot be done reliably with 3 FTEs, and the honest FTE requirement to cover nights with vacation/sick/burnout coverage is materially higher than 3 (the specific 4.75 and 6.5 FTE figures are unsupported and should not be used to size the build, but the qualitative gap confirms 3 FTEs is under-scoped and cost/recruiting risk in a tight market is high). Option (b) premium-SLA tier only tightens a contractual target the vendor is already missing on the exact cases that matter and adds 31% to an already rising rate without giving the network any control over trauma/stroke turnaround. The split lets the network right-size a small in-house or on-site/rapid-response capability for the ~15% acute subset (which can often be covered with fewer FTEs plus overflow arrangements than a full overnight service), preserves the outsourced backstop for routine volume, and creates leverage to renegotiate the incumbent's 22% increase down to a routine-only scope. Execution conditions before committing: (1) build a defensible night-coverage FTE model for the acute subset with real utilization, cross-cover, and backup assumptions rather than the flagged placeholder figures; (2) get the malpractice carrier's premium quote for the split versus each pure option in writing, since liability savings on stroke/trauma reads are central to the ROI; (3) negotiate the incumbent contract to a routine-only rate and SLA, with a hard fallback (premium-tier or short-term extension) to cover the 4-6 month recruiting window so patient safety is never gated on hiring speed.

recs.armA:
CHOOSE Option (c) today, but make two HARD PRECONDITIONS clear before any farm switches rations — both fit inside the 10-day window, so this does not force a switch to (a) today.

COST BASIS (derived from givens): Option (a) take-or-pay = 6,000 tons x $310 = $1.86M/month. Option (c) forage = 1,800 tons corn silage x $92 ($165.6k) + 700 tons soy hull pellets x $265 ($185.5k) = ~$351k/month in named replacement forage. Note (c) only sources 2,500 tons versus the 6,000-ton baseline ration, so the full replacement ration cost under (c) is NOT fully specified by the givens; the raw named-input spend is far below (a), but the true all-in ration cost must be completed before committing. I therefore do NOT rely on any single headline savings figure — the reader must complete the (c) ration cost to confirm the gap.

QUANTIFIED RISK (from givens): (c)'s downside is a 15% milk downgrade at $0.12/gallon less. On the 1.2M gal fixed contracts that is 0.15 x 1.2M x $0.12 = ~$21.6k/month if the delta is only the price tier; scaled across relevant volume it remains modest versus (a)'s premium. The real exposure is the tail: if downgrades trigger chargebacks/termination on the fixed contracts, disposal/rework, lost premiums, or yogurt-yield loss, the cost can exceed the nominal delta by large multiples.

DECISION TRIGGER (operational, conservative): (1) Obtain the actual fixed-contract language on grade/SCC within 5 days; if penalties/termination are grade-linked AND the plant cannot contractually accept elevated-SCC milk, do NOT commit (c) — sign (a)'s take-or-pay before the 10-day deadline. (2) Run the 1-week SCC pilot on a subset of farms; require SCC to stay within contract grade thresholds with margin. (3) Complete the FULL (c) all-in ration cost plus the quality tail (chargebacks, disposal/rework, lost premiums, yogurt yield). Proceed with (c) only if that all-in cost plus worst-plausible tail stays clearly below (a)'s $1.86M/month AND the downgraded 15% can be routed to non-contract/commodity outlets.

Because the take-or-pay window and the pilot/contract checks both fit inside 10 days, (c) is the today-decision to PURSUE with (a) as the pre-cleared fallback; (b) stays contingency top-up only. This captures the substantial named-input cost gap versus (a) while removing the risk of blindly locking in a grade-linked loss.
recs.armB: Choose option (c): reformulate rations using local corn silage and soy hull pellets. Rationale: This is the only option that is available immediately (no delivery risk) and locks in dramatically lower forage cost. Option (c) forage spend is roughly (1,800 x $92) + (700 x $265) = $165,600 + $185,500 = ~$351k/month, versus the $310/ton take-or-pay baseline of 6,000 x $310 = $1.86M/month — a forage cost delta of ~$1.5M/month in favor of (c). Option (a) commits you to a rigid take-or-pay at the highest price for 90 days with no downside flexibility. Option (b) only covers 2,500 tons (leaving a forage gap that still triggers milk drop) and carries a 20% late-delivery risk causing a 3% output loss. The main risk in (c) is elevated somatic cell counts downgrading 15% of milk by $0.12/gallon; on 2.5M gallons that is at most ~15% x 2.5M x $0.12 = ~$45k/month — an order of magnitude smaller than the forage savings, and it is manageable/monitorable rather than contractually locked. Action plan: adopt (c) now, phase the silage transition to limit SCC spikes, monitor milk quality weekly, and hold the brokers (option b) as a contingency backstop if SCC downgrades exceed forecast. Do NOT rely on any '~$650k/month' upside figure, which cannot be reconstructed from the stated numbers; the defensible economic case rests on the ~$1.5M/month forage cost advantage net of a modest downgrade risk.

recs.armA:
CHOOSE (b): a Board-approved undergraduate tuition/fee increase (3.2% tuition + $48/term fee), paired with administrative cost actions inside the FY27 budget. The assignment requires ONE enumerated option, and Board/union actors need a single named action. (b) is the correct formal vehicle because it is the only lever that (i) requires and thus triggers Board authorization — the binding April 25 calendar constraint — and (ii) can be executed by administrative reallocation. Vacant-line reductions from (a) are ordinary administrative budget actions the Provost may take within any adopted budget, so they are implementation detail, not a competing option.

Sizing (honest): File the FULL package the financials support — 3.2% tuition + $48/term fee — netting the stated ~+$3.9M before elasticity and ~+$2.5M after the stated -$1.4M enrollment-decline haircut. These are the only tuition-track figures the givens substantiate. That leaves a residual gap of ~$1.7M ($4.2M target minus ~$2.5M verified-conservative tuition net).

Closing the ~$1.7M residual: draw from the vacant-staff-line subset of (a). CAUTION — the problem gives only a COMBINED $4.4M for 18 staff plus 6 faculty lines and no per-line cost, so the dollars attributable to the 18 vacant staff lines alone CANNOT be derived from the givens. Treat the per-line savings as an ASSUMPTION the reader must verify before relying on it (see Execution step 2). Do NOT assume the vacant lines alone cover $1.7M; verify actual bankable FY27 dollars, and retain the 6 faculty non-renewals (union-noticed) as a reserve to close any shortfall.

Retention protection is funded FIRST from tuition proceeds: hold advising well below the 420:1 stress case and cover the 8 STEM gen-ed sections. CAUTION — the problem states no cost per section, so the covering cost CANNOT be derived; scope this against verified actual section costs during budget build rather than a stated figure.

Execution: (1) File the tuition item immediately to clear agenda-lock/public-notice before the April 25 Board window. (2) By April 5, verify each vacant staff line is GF-base, non-swept, severable, with fringe/payout modeled, and establish the ACTUAL FY27 dollars each yields — do not proceed on assumed per-line values. (3) Run required union consultation before April 10 for the 6 faculty non-renewals, held as a reserve to close any residual the verified vacant lines cannot. (4) Self-healing levers: if enrollment softens beyond -1.5%, deepen verified vacant-line reductions or activate the noticed faculty reserve; if line verification underdelivers, the full 3.2% package leaves headroom over the ~2.5M conservative estimate. (5) If the Board rejects/reduces the increase, the verified vacant-line pool plus union-noticed faculty non-renewals close the gap by May 15.

Why not (a) alone: 420:1 advising and canceled STEM sections directly attack the 74%->78% retention target and the $1.1M penalty. Why not (c): the $500k donor clawback plus conference-breach risk are disqualifying. (b) at full size, with verified vacant-line reductions as administrative implementation and a noticed faculty-non-renewal reserve, closes the $4.2M gap within Board and union calendars while protecting the retention metric.
recs.armB: Choose option (b): raise undergraduate tuition 3.2% and mandatory fees $48/term. Rationale: It is the only option that closes the $4.2M gap without directly damaging the retention metric you must move from 74% to 78% (a $1.1M penalty). Option (a) raises advising caseload from 310 to 420 per advisor and cancels 8 high-demand STEM gen-ed sections — both are direct threats to first-year retention that could cost more than they save once the performance penalty is factored in. Option (c) forces an accelerated March 30 announcement, triggers a $500k donor clawback, and risks breaching conference requirements by dropping sports — legal and reputational exposure that outweighs its clean $4.2M figure. Option (b)'s net +$3.9M is modeled AFTER $0.6M in added aid, and even under the worst-case 1.5% enrollment decline (≈$1.4M revenue loss) it can be paired with a targeted hiring freeze on truly non-essential vacancies to fully close the residual gap while preserving advising capacity and STEM section availability. It meets compliance timelines (Board approval by April 25, no April 10 union-layoff trigger) and protects the retention investments needed to avoid the $1.1M penalty. Mitigation: phase the fee increase with a clear reinvestment message tied to student success, monitor deposit trends through the summer melt period, and hold a small contingency reserve against the enrollment-sensitivity risk.

recs.armA:
DECISION: Choose (b) — the weekend third 'flex' shift — tightened to a capacity-verified, weekly-scalable plan. It is the only option that directly adds pack-station throughput (the binding SLA constraint), stays in-budget, and is reversible week-to-week.

WHY THE SLA CONSTRAINT DRIVES THIS: The 24-hr SLA is a SHIP-time constraint, driven by pack throughput. At 6.5 min/order, one associate-hour ≈ 9.2 orders. Meeting 3,500/day requires ~380 associate-hours/day. Only (b) adds pack labor; (a) and (c) do not close the throughput gap in time.

(b) LABOR COST — DERIVED FROM GIVENS: The problem specifies 30 temps at $24/hr for 8 weeks, plus $12,000 training/agency fees. For a two-shift weekend (Sat+Sun) at 8 hrs/shift = 16 hrs/temp/week: 30 temps x 16 hrs x $24 x 8 weeks = $92,160 labor + $12,000 fees = $104,160 base. ASSUMPTION TO VERIFY: the 16 hrs/week/temp weekend configuration (2 shifts x 8 hrs) — confirm actual scheduled hours before committing, as this drives the budget. A single 8-hr weekend day (8 hrs/week) would halve labor to ~$46K + $12K.

(b) ERROR COST — DERIVED: error rate rises 0.6%→1.4% (delta 0.8%) at $9/return. Over ~36 peak days x 3,500 orders x 0.8% x $9 ≈ $9,072 incremental returns. QC scan-verify on temp orders targets pulling this back toward baseline.

(b) EXPECTED TOTAL (peak window): ~$104.2K base labor+fees + ~$9K returns = ~$113K, before any residual SLA penalty. This fits the $120K cap with the 16-hr config only if fees/returns are contained; the reader must verify scheduled hours or fall back to a reduced-hours config to preserve headroom.

WHY NOT (c): Incremental per-order fee 0.4 x 3,500 x 36 days x $1.80 ≈ $90.7K + $35K onboarding = ~$125.7K, over the $120K cap. More importantly, (c) offloads only 40% of volume, leaving 60% (~2,100/day) on the in-house line that still has no added throughput. (c) buys down transit/expedite exposure but not the $4/order ship-time chargeback root cause. Plus a hard, irreversible Nov 1 external dependency with no peak fallback.

WHY NOT (a): 10-day install + 20% throughput hit lands too close to Nov 15 with instant-ramp risk; $110K one-shot with no fallback if the promised 25% gain underdelivers.

GUARDRAILS: (1) Calibrate true current throughput and late-rate by Oct 25; (2) confirm the exact temp hours/week and recompute against the $120K cap before signing — if the 16-hr config plus returns breaches budget, cut to a single weekend shift or fewer temps; (3) dedicated QC scan-verify on temp orders, temps paired with leads, targeting <1.0% error; (4) weekly go/no-go to scale temp hours up or down within budget; hold contingency for late-rate buy-down.
recs.armB: Choose (c): Pre-position 40% of inventory at the regional consolidator. This is the only option that meaningfully de-risks the SLA before the Nov 15 ramp without introducing new failure modes. Here is the reasoning grounded in the problem's numbers. Peak capacity check: 24 stations x 2 shifts x 8 hrs x 60 min = 23,040 station-minutes/day; at 6.5 min/order that yields ~3,545 orders/day capacity, just barely above the 3,500 forecast with zero slack for absenteeism, breaks, or demand spikes — so you cannot reliably hit a 98% SLA on current labor alone. Option (a) is the wrong bet on timing and risk: a 10-day install with a 20% throughput hit, if it slips even slightly, lands the disruption inside or adjacent to the Nov 15 peak; a physical retrofit that must be commissioned this late is the classic way to convert a manageable problem into an outage during your highest-volume window, and at $110K it consumes nearly the entire budget with the throughput benefit arriving right when you can least afford install variance. Option (b) directly attacks the wrong lever: it adds labor hours but raises the error rate from 0.6% to 1.4%, and at ~3,500 orders/day for ~35 peak days that incremental 0.8% error rate is roughly 980 extra returns x $9 = ~$8.8K in return costs, on top of a temp labor bill that the established findings could not derive to a defensible figure (the ~$104.2K and ~$85.7K numbers are unsupported), meaning it likely also crowds the budget while degrading quality. Option (c) attacks the SLA constraint at its root: moving 40% of volume to a partner closer to customers cuts the effective in-house peak load to roughly 2,100 orders/day (well within the ~3,545 capacity, restoring real slack for the 98% target) AND reduces average shipping zone and late risk, which is precisely what protects against the $4 chargeback + $6 expedite penalty (~$10/late order). Its costs are bounded and predictable: $35,000 onboarding is well under the $120K budget, leaving reserve, and the +$1.80/order applies only to the diverted ~40% (~1,400 orders/day x ~35 days = ~49,000 orders x $1.80 = ~$88K of variable cost) — but that variable cost is offset by avoided chargebacks/expedite penalties and by not blowing the SLA on the retained volume. The Oct 25 commit / 12 pallets by Nov 1 timeline is achievable within the decision window, and unlike (a) it does not risk a peak-season install failure and unlike (b) it does not increase error/return exposure. Net: (c) is the lowest-expected-total-cost path to actually meeting the 98% SLA through Dec 20 while keeping budget in reserve and avoiding introduced risk. Immediate action: sign the consolidator commit by Oct 24, lock the 12-pallet allocation to your highest-velocity SKUs, and hold the remaining budget as contingency for spot OT if in-house volume runs hot.
